what_it_does: Renders the active walkthrough/review card. It switches between a guided tour (narration, spotlight, step-through, no verdicts) and a review session (framing, reason input, verdict buttons, next step), while disabling all actions during a pending request and offering a voice/text toggle.
observations: Uses useApp from AppContext for session, voiceOn, wtReason, wtBusy, wtSpoke, setVoiceOn, setWtReason, endWalk, resolveUiTarget, respondStep, nextStep.
Returns null when there is no active session.
Branches into guide mode when session.guide or session.raw.guide_address is truthy.
Shares a common header (title, voice/text toggle, progress, close) across both modes.
Guide mode shows a 'showing this part of the screen' target, narration, and a Next button but no verdict row or reason input.
Review mode shows framing with fallbacks, a UI target line, a reason input, verdict buttons (approve, reject, comment, skip, decide), and a Next button.
Every respond control and the Next button are disabled while wtBusy is true.
data-ui-ref attributes are preserved on many elements for UI testing/spotlighting.
Inline comments document concurrency guards, fail-safe framing, and operator-only verdicts.
standouts: Single component handles both a guided tour and an item-review workflow.
Uniform wtBusy guard applied to all action buttons to prevent concurrent clicks.
Inline comments are dense and encode design decisions/constraints (F0, C1, S7b).
Includes a 'decide' verdict that claims deterministic resolution.
conventions: React functional component with an early null return.
CSS class names prefixed with wt-.
Inline comments use markers like F0, C1, S7b.
Uses logical OR fallbacks for missing framing or target values.
concerns: Inline styles are mixed with className in several places.
The reason input is only required by placeholder text; no visible runtime validation enforces it for reject/comment.
Relies on session.raw shape without local type guards.
The 'decide' verdict's deterministic behavior is asserted in a title but not evidenced locally.
notes: A single UI card that doubles as both a guided tour and a review walkthrough, with a global busy lock preventing double-clicks.
questions: What does respondStep('decide') actually trigger on the backend?
Is wtBusy set for both respond and next operations, or only respond actions?
How is voiceOn consumed by the text-to-speech driver elsewhere?
What is the exact TypeScript shape of session.raw and where is it enforced?
purpose_vs_actual: The stated purpose is the visible review organ for an active session; it matches, and additionally extends the same card to serve as a guided tour.
apparent_themes: Guided onboarding via backend-addressed interface elements.
intention_signals: To give operators a bounded, non-blocking way to review items or learn the interface, ensuring one action at a time and surfacing machine addresses only as test hooks rather than user-facing labels.
novelty: The same card component collapses a guided tour and a review workflow, reusing layout and concurrency controls while explicitly excluding verdicts from the tour path.
contribution: A self-contained, dual-mode walkthrough card with uniform busy-state guarding, voice/text toggle, backend-driven spotlight targets, and clear per-step affordances for both learning and review.
summary_for_embedding: Walkthrough.tsx is a React component that renders the active walkthrough card, supporting both a guided tour mode and a review mode with verdict controls, busy-state concurrency guards, voice/text toggle, and backend-driven UI spotlighting.
intention_for_embedding: Provide a single, robust operator-facing card for stepping through guided interface tours or reviewing items and recording verdicts without concurrent request races.

symbols.does: Renders the active walkthrough/review card with guide and review modes.
symbols.description: A React functional component that pulls session and walkthrough controls from AppContext. It branches between a guided tour (narration, spotlight, next step, no verdicts) and a review session (framing, reason input, approve/reject/comment/skip/decide verdicts, next step), disables all action buttons while wtBusy is true, and exposes data-ui-ref hooks for UI tests.
